titleStmt.title: C.—Schedule of claims presented to the commission by claimants against the respective governments, with indexes to same..

div.head: C.—Schedule of claims presented to the commission by claimants against the respective governments, with indexes to same.

row.cell: Cotton used by United States Army
Furniture destroyed by United States Army.
Use of and damage to real estate and personal property used and destroyed by United States Army.
Use and destruction of personal property by United States Army.
With $15,000 per annum to be added, with interest at 7 per cent, per annum until paid.

Mary S. Hill
(Same case as No. 198.)
Dismissed without prejudice to file new memorial Nov. 15, 1871. Award signed Aug. 28, 1873.
Use of and damage to real estate by United States Army.
Furniture, &c., destroyed by United States Army.
Illegal imprisonment and personal property taken by United States Army.
Personal property used and destroyed by United States Army.
Value of personal property (State bank stock, &c.) destroyed by the war.
Cotton taken by United States and sold; net proceeds received through Court of Claims.
Dismissed for want of jurisdiction, and without prejudice, Nov. 29, 1872.
Destruction of dwelling-house and personal property by United States Army.
Cotton, cattle, and other personal property used and destroyed by United States Anny.
Clothing taken by United States Army.
Value of confederate cotton loan bond No. 499. series C.
Horses, corn, hogs, hay, &c., taken, used, and destroyed by the United States Army.
Sugar, molasses, and rum, taken and sold by the United States. Case had been on docket of Court of Claims and United States Supreme Court.
Corn, hogs, poultry, saddles, bridles, tools, &c., taken, used, and destroyed by United States Army.
Use of and destruction of real estate, drugs, medicines, paints, oils, &c., by United States Army.
Illegal imprisonment by United States, loss of profits from business, and loss of personal property.
Mules, corn, bacon, flour, cattle, clothing, &c., taken by United States Army.
Furniture, clothing, provisions, &c., burned by United States Army.
Tobacco destroyed by United States Army.
Two horses taken by United States Army.
Beef taken and used by the United States Army.
Lucy J. Park, administratrix of W. T. Bruce.
Dwelling-house burned and personal property taken, used, and destroyed by Hinted States Army.
Use of and destruction of real estate, furniture, &c., by United States Army.
One horse taken by United States Army.
Loss on 29 bales of cotton burned by rebels.
78 bales of cotton burned by United States Army.
For rent of house and repair of damage thereto by United States Army.
Rent of store, damage to goods, and damage to business, as dealer in queensware, &c., by the United States Army.
23 bales of cotton seized by United States.
Award for $5,333 Sent, 8, 1873; 4th item of claim withdrawn Mar. 11, 1873.
100 bales of cotton burned by United States Army.
300 bales of cotton, part seized and part burned by United States Army.
81 bales of cotton seized by United States.
Tobacco seized by United States Army.
70 bales of cotton seized by United States Army.
Money seized by United States Army.
Corn, horses, mules, cattle, hogs, &c., seized by United States Army.
Three horses seized by United States Army.
Pictures and frames destroyed by fire by United States Army.
Cows, fences, hogs, &c., taken and used by United States Army.
135 bales of cotton, burned by United States Army.
Destruction of dwelling, stable, and corn-house; appropriation of horse, cattle, fence-rails, wood, &c., by United States Army.
31 bales of cotton, taken by United States.
81 bales of cotton, taken by United States.
Cotton burned by United States Army.
Dwelling and out-houses destroyed and timber used by United States Army.

Executors of R. S. C. A. Alexander.
(Was born in U. S.)
Injury to real estate by United States Army, and destroying out-houses, fences, trees, &c.
Dismissed for want of jurisdiction April 23, 1872.
Bacon, flour, cattle, &c., taken and used by United States Army.
Iron, lumber, blacksmith’s tools, use of shop, &c., by United States Army.
Damage to dwelling, fences, trees, steam-engine; use arid occupation of dwelling, and damage to vault and coffin by United States Army.
Lumber taken and used, and destruction of personal effects and damage to real estate by United States Army.
Dwelling-house and factory used by the United States Army.
Mules, carts, and harness taken and used by United States Army.
Rev. Frederick W. Boyd (a naturalized British subject).
Dismissed for want of jurisdiction March 22, 1873.
20 cattle, corn, horses, &c., taken and used by United States Army.
Tobacco destroyed by United States Army.
Two horses and watch and chain taken by United States Army.
John N. Trook, administrator of Wm. Hughes.
Use of farm, damage to house, fences, trees, and use of vegetables, corn, oats, &c., by United States Army.
Cattle taken and used by United States Army.
Illegal imprisonment by United States, loss of property, business, and credit.
Illegal imprisonment, loss by sale of land and horses taken by United States Army.
Tobacco and other goods, &c., taken, used, and destroyed by United States Army.
Destruction of machinery, material, and building; rent, and damage and loss by United States Army.
2 horses taken by United States Army
1 horse taken by United States Army
Money and merchandise taken by United States Army.
Gun-carriages, &c., taken by United States Army.
Sailing and flat boat burned by United States Army.
2 horses taken by United States Army
Rum destroyed by United States Arm
Cotton taken and sold by United State
Flour, sugar, bacon, molasses, tobacco, wearing apparel, &c., taken and used by United States Army.
Cotton seized and sold by United States Claim pending in Court of Claims.
Dismissed for want of jurisdiction, without prejudice to the prosecution of the claim elsewhere. January 20, 1873.
Cotton seized and sold by United States. Claim pending in Court of Claims.
Dismissed for want of jurisdiction, without prejudice to the prosecution of the claim elsewhere, January 20, 1873.
Woolen factory, stock, and fixtures destroyed by the United States Army.
Corn, hay, cattle, bacon, &c., taken and used by United States Army.
Martha A. Rayne, administratrix of Robert Parker Rayne.
Cotton seized by United States Army; part of claim on Court of Claims docket.
Court of Claims case withdrawn November 30, 1872, and rest of claim disallowed August 14, 1873.
Furniture, clothing, and provisions taken and used by United States Army.
Starch, 1 horse, bacon, &c., taken, used, and destroyed by United States Army, and damage to and suspension of business.
Destruction of dwelling and outhouses, fences, and orchards, and the taking and using cows, hogs, hay, &c., by United States Army.
One horse, corn, fodder, watches, &c., taken by United States Army.
Patrick Eagan, administrator of William Donnelly.

Cotton taken and sold by United States;
                            whisky and rum taken and used by United States Army.
Cotton claim
                            was on docket Court of Claims.
Award for $2,334 April 28, 1873; claim as to cotton dismissed without prejudice.
Lumber taken and used by United States Army, and damage to business.
Award for $1,440 December 10, 1872, and $25 more January 15, 1873.
Hay, corn, wheat, oats, &c., taken and used by United States Army, and damage to barn, orchard, and stable.
Five mules taken by United States Army.
Destruction of house, fencing, and cistern, and loss of rent, by United States Army.
Dwelling-house and store, stock of goods, tools, and fixtures, taken, used, and damaged by United States Army.
Corn, bacon tobacco, horses, furniture, &c., taken, used, and destroyed by United States Army.
48 bales of cotton, taken and sold by United States.
125 bales of cotton, burned by United States Army.

Linseed-oil, seized and used by United States.
Received net proceeds of cotton seized through Court of Claims.
Furniture, tobacco, Virginia banknotes, &c., destroyed by United States Army.
Cotton burned by United States Army
Mules, horses, cattle, and wood taken and used, and damage to dwelling-house. &c., by United States Army.
Cotton burned by United States Army.
Horses, mules, corn, &c., taken by United States Army
Horses taken by United States Army.
Cotton, mules, horses, &c., taken by United States.
Buildings, furniture, &c., burned by United States Army.
1 horse and 3 mules, wood, bay, and oats, taken and used by United States Army, and nursery destroyed.
Horses, cattle, &c., taken by Missouri State militia.
Sugar taken and used by United States Army.
Tarpaulings taken by United States Army.
Millinery goods taken, used, and destroyed by United States Army.
Fencing and crop destroyed by United States Army.
Damage to and destruction of property by United States Army, and vegetables taken and used.
220 bales of cotton burned by United States Army.
Claim for loss on cotton seized withdrawn August 23, 1873 and rest of claim disallowed September 16, 1873.
895 bales of cotton burned by United States Army.
250 bales of cotton burned by United States Army.
Loss on cotton seized by United States.
Store sacked and goods destroyed by United States Army.
Occupation of house and lots and damage to coal-boats by United States Army.
Cotton burned by United States Army
Cotton seized by United States Army, and claim on docket of C’t of Claims.
Sugar and molasses destroyed by United States Army.
Sugar destroyed by the United States Army.
School broken up; books, maps, &c. destroyed; horse, &c., taken by United States Army.
Flour taken and used by United States Army.
Illegal imprisonment and money taken from him by United States Army.
Cotton taken and used by United States Army.
Tobacco, cotton, horse, taken, used, and destroyed by United States Army.
Sugar taken by United States Army
Horses, cattle, corn, hay, &c., taken, used, and destroyed by United States Army.

Corn, hay, oats mules, horse, &c., taken, used, and destroyed by United States Army.
Became a naturalized United States citizen September 19, 1872.
One mule, hogs, cow and calf, bacon, &c., taken and used by United States.
Dwelling and out-houses, corn, fodder, and cotton burned by United States Army.
23 bales of cotton burned by United States Army.
62 bales of cotton burned by United States Army.
Loss of salt-works and machinery; peril of life, &c., by United States Army.
Horses, hay, tobacco, wine, liquors, &c., taken and used by United States Army: loss of business, &c.
Flour, sugar, tobacco, wine, liquors, &c., taken and used by United States Army, and destruction of his business.
Patterson iron-works, machinery, and tools taken by United States.
Damage to general business for eleven months.
Dwelling-house and saw-mill, lumber, iron, coal, ferry-boat, cattle, household furniture, taken, used, and destroyed by United States Army.
Rent of dwelling-house and buildings used by United States Army.
Destruction of orchard by United States Army.
Destruction of fence by United States Army.
27 bales of cotton seized by United States Army.
Horses, cows, hogs, corn, flour, &c., taken, used, and destroyed by United States Army.

Thomas Barry
(Claimant died, and case was revived in the name of Roderick Seal, administrator.)
Sugar and merchandise taken, used, and destroyed by United States Army.
Sugar taken and used by United States Army.
Destruction of dwellings, stables, &c., corn, horses, &c., taken, used, and destroyed by United States Army.
Household furniture, &c., taken, used, and destroyed by United States Army.
Tobacco, rice, flour, &c., taken and used by United States Army.
Dwelling and out-houses, fencing, orchard, &c., destroyed by United States Army.
Horses, beds, blankets, corn, &c., taken and used by United States Army, and illegal imprisonment by United States.
Horses, mules, corn, wood, &c., household furniture, vegetables, &c., taken and used by United States Army.
Horse, cows, hay, groceries, &c., taken and used by United States Army.
Cotton, corn, horses, cattle, mules, &c., taken, used, and destroyed by United States Army.
Bacon, lard, sugar, horses, mules, cattle, hogs, corn, &c., taken and used by United States Army.
Dwelling and out-houses, cotton, and blacksmith’s tools, &c., destroyed by United States Array.
Drug-store, stock of drugs, bank notes, horses, mules, &c., taken, used, and destroyed by United States Army.
Eliza B. Nelson (“Later Isaac B. Cornwall and Eliza B. Cornwall.)
Use, occupation, and damage to real estate by United States Army.
Horse, mule, corn, vegetables, sugar, &c., taken and used by United States Army.
Arrest and bad treatment by United States Army.
Wm. K. Prior administrator of John Pryor.
Horses, mules, tobacco, rum, sugar, store goods, &c., taken and used by United States Army.
Cattle, hogs, wood, timber cut, bricks, taken and used by United States Army; illegal imprisonment by United States; damage to practice, &c.
Cotton burned by United States Army.
Horses, mules, corn, &c., taken by United States Army.
Stock of drugs, fixtures, &c., destroyed by United States Army.
Cotton taken by United States Army.
One cow, hogs, tools, furniture, &c., taken, used, and destroyed by United States Army.
Occupation and damage to dwelling-house and lot, by United States Army.
Brig Napier and cargo, seized and sold by United States. No appeal to Supreme Court.
Off Cape Fear River, N. C.
Cotton burned by United States Army.
Four mules, cow and calf, fencing, &c., taken and used by United States Army.
Wine, whisky, furniture, fowls, &c., taken, used, and destroyed by United States Army.
Sugar, molasses, &c., seized by United States Army.
One horse taken by United States Army.
Money, tobacco, and brandy, seized by United States Army.
Dwelling-house destroyed by United States Army.
Furniture, clothing, &c., taken by United States Army.
Damage by loss of house by United States Army.
Brigantine Madeira run into and sunk by United States steamer Clyde.
Anna C. Grougan, executrix of Park R. Grougan.
Goods taken, used, and destroyed by United States Army.
Illegal imprisonment, and horse taken by United States Army.
Loss on cotton seized by United States.
Ann Hayes, wife of a United States citizen.
Dwelling-house and furniture destroyed by United States Army.
Dismissed for want of jurisdiction, and without prejudice. Feb. 7. 1873.
Edward J. Bennett, alias Arto Croger.
Illegal imprisonment by United States; money, clothing, &c., taken from him by United States.
Cotton, horses, mules, cattle, &c., taken and used by United States Army.
Cotton seized and sold by United States. Claim pending in Court of Claims.
Dismissed for want of jurisdiction and without prejudice, Dec. 5, 1872.
Horses, cattle, hay, corn, potatoes, poultry, furniture, &c., taken, used, and destroyed, by United States Army.
Furniture and library burned by United States Army.
Elizabeth S. Thorns, administratrix of David Thorns.
Use of house, use of and damage to furniture and household goods by United States Army.
For loss of wages and expenses incurred by seizure of brig Dolphin by United States steamer Wachusetts, and loss of clothing, nautical instruments. &c.
Seizure and detention of cargo of Isabella Thompson by United States steamer United States.
Seizure and detention of brigantine Isabella Thompson by United States steamer United States.
Mules, clothing, &c., taken and used by United States Army.
19 mules and 1 horse taken by United States Army.
Tan-bark burned by United States Army.
Lot of tools destroyed by United States Army, and loss in business.
Dismissed for want of jurisdiction Oct. 3, 1872.
Dwelling-house, fence, and furniture, destroyed by United States Army.
Cotton seized by United States Army.
Illegal imprisonment by United States, and horse, &c., taken by United States Army.
Cotton seized and sold by United States
Seizure of brig Ariel and cargo, captured by United States steamer Gemsbok, and sold by United States. No anneal to Supreme Court.
Cotton, warehouse, &c., burned by United States Army.
Jane L. Brand, widow of a United States citizen.
Illegal imprisonment and expulsion by United States; furniture and clothing used and destroyed by United States Army.
Dismissed without prejudice to the prosecution of the claim elsewhere, June 20, 1873.
One horse, cattle, poultry, furniture, &c., taken, used, and destroyed by United States Army.
Vegetables, fruits, hay, one horse, &c., taken and used by United States Army.
Norton Marshall, executor of James Gem mill.
Flour taken and used by United States Army.
Martha M. Tooraen, widow of a United States citizen.
Dwelling and out-houses, furniture, clothing, and stock of goods, warehouse and fixtures burned by United States Navy.
Dismissed for want of jurisdiction March 31, 1873.
Horses, cows, and coal taken, used, and destroyed by United States Army.
Carriage-factory and blacksmith-shop, destroyed by United States Army.
Corn, horses, fodder, bacon, molasses, cattle, sheep, hogs &c., taken and used by United States Army.
Eliza J. Jay, administratrix of James Jay.
Lumber taken and used by United States Army.
Hogs, sheep, horses, mules, poultry, corn, hay, cedar rails, &c., taken and destroyed by United States Army.
Occupation of land, damage to dwelling and out-houses, &c., by United States Army.
Furniture, &c., burned by United States Army.
Horses, mules, cattle, sheep, poultry, bacon, corn, rice, furniture, &c., taken, used, and destroyed by United States Army.
Loss of crops on farm, two and a half years.
Furniture, books, and provisions burned by United States Army.
Thomas S. Mahen, administrator of Stephen McLean.
Cotton, cattle, hogs, 1 mule taken and used by United States Army.
No British beneficiary and proved and disallowed Sept. 24, 1873.
Dwelling-house, furniture, clothing, &c., burned by United States Army.
Cotton burned by United States Army.
Photographic building, fixtures, &c., used and destroyed by United States Army.
Loss and damage to his business
Illegal imprisonment by the United States.
Illegal seizure of Tubal Cain by United States.
Furniture, &c., burned by United States Army.
For timber cut and used by United States Army.
Furniture, &c., taken and destroyed, and illegal arrest by United States Army.
Wood, corn, rope, bagging, and twine taken and used by United States Army.
James B. Halley, administrator of Jas. Pollock.
Cotton, corn, bacon, &c., taken and used by United States Army.
Cotton taken and used by United States Army.
Cotton taken and used by United States Army.
Sugar and molasses taken and sold by United States.
Isaac Milner, claimant, died, and case was revived in the name of Joanna R. Milner, administratrix.
Cotton burned by United States Army.
One horse, wheat, bacon, corn, beef, &c., taken and used by United States Army.
One mare, 1 jack, poultry, rails, &c., taken and used by United States Army.
Sugar, tobacco, horse, &c., taken by United States Army.
Parish of West Baton Rouge. La.
Parish of West Baton Rouge, La.
Money, furniture, &c., taken, used, and destroyed by United States Army.
Tobacco burned by United States Army.
Tobacco captured by United States vessels.
John C. Ferris, administ’r of John Donohue Smith.
School-furniture, books, fences, house, &c., taken, used, and destroyed, and loss of business, by United States Army.
Stock of goods, furniture, &c., taken, used, and destroyed, and damage to business and goods, by United States Army.
Molasses on schooner Pacifique, captured by United States steamer Stars and Stripes. No appeal to Supreme Court.
Schooner Pacifique, captured by United States Steamer Stars and Stripes. No appeal to Supreme Court.
Ship Boyne, warned off the whole southern coast by United States steamship Niagara.
Cotton burned by United States Army.
Mules, sugar, and furniture used and destroyed by United States Army.
Boots, shoes, dry-goods, &c., used and destroyed by United States Army.
Damage for loss of business, health, &c
Cotton seized and destroyed by United States Army.
Hay, corn, oats, mules, cows, hogs, poultry, furniture, &c., taken and used by United States Army.
Injury to person by a United States soldier.
Two horses, 1 mule, 1 cow, 3 hogs, poultry, &c., taken and used by United States Army.
Rice, flour, tobacco, sugar, United States currency, &c., taken and used by United States Army.
Lead mine and fixtures, store-house, stock of goods, &c.; illegal imprisonment, loss of business, &c., caused by United States Army.
Tobacco seized and used by United States Army.
Cotton seized and sold by United States, and interest on $11,050.70 to date of decree of Court of Claims; net proceeds received through Court of Claims.
Dismissed for want of jurisdiction, and without prejudice, Nov. 29, 1872.
Saw-mill and lumber taken by United States Army.
Two houses burned by United States Army.
Tobacco, cloth, coffee, sugar, blankets, flannel, whisky, wines, liquors, dry-goods, &c., burned by U. S. Army.
Saw and grist mill and fixtures, dwelling and out-houses destroyed by United States Army; bacon, sugar, corn-meal. &c., taken and used by United States Army.
Horses, mules, cattle, hogs, bacon, molasses, furniture, &c., taken, used, and destroyed by U. S. Army.
Mules, cows, furniture, &c., taken, used and destroyed by U. S. Army.
For mortgage-lien on property burned by United States Army.
Charles Coleman; claimant died, and case was revived in the name of L. L. L. Coleman, administratrix.
Coffee captured in the Sally Magee by United States steamer Quaker City.
One horse and 25,000 pounds tobacco taken and used by U. S. Army.
One horse, saddle and bridle, boots, shoes, cattle, hogs, and corn, taken and used by United States Army; house and furniture burned by United States Army.
Diamonds, gold and silver watches, United States currency, bank-bills, cotton, tobacco, liquors, general merchandise, house and hotel furniture, &c., burned and used by United States Army.
Cotton taken and destroyed by United States Army.
Coal taken by United States steamer Vanderbilt.
Timber cut adrift, lost, or destroyed by United States Army.
Tobacco seized snd burned by United States Army.
Alice Barton, executrix, and Wm. Cogan, executor, of Richard C. Barton.
Fish taken and used by United States Army.
Edwin Gerard, assignee of T. A. Corry J. Laycock, and A. and S. Henry & Co.
Sir William Peel, captured by United States steamer Seminole.
Edwin Gerard, assignee of certain underwriters at Lloyds.
Part of cargo of Dashing Wave, captured by United States steamers Owasco and Virginia.

Edwin Gerard, assignee for the Universal Marino Insurance Company, of London; also of the liquidators-of the English and Scottish Marine Insurance Company, of London; also of certain underwriters at Lloyds; also attorney in fact for the British and Foreign Marine Inrance Company, of Liverpool.
Brig Volant’s cargo, captured by United States steamer Virginia.
Wood, timber, fruit-trees, cows, horse, hogs, mules, &c., &c., taken, used, and destroyed by United States Army, and damage to store.
Two horses, taken and used by United States Army.
Robert H. Watt, administrator of Hugh Watt.
Cattle, mules, horses, hogs, corn, molasses, sugar, bacon, sheep, and cotton taken and used by United States Army.
Furniture, &c., burned by United States Army.
Dwelling and workshop, stock and material, burned by United States Army.
Elizab’h Armstrong, administratrix of Robert Armstrong.
Horses, cattle, flour, furniture, merchandise, &c., taken, used, and destroyed, and store-house and warehouse burned by United States Army.
Stock of goods (queensware and general grocery) taken and used by United States Army.
Stock of goods (dry goods, groceries, &c.) and dwelling-house, burned by United States Army.
Claim for vouchers withdrawn May 5, 1873, and rest of claim disallowed Aug. 19, 1873.
Dwelling-house, corn, fodder, one horse, furniture, flour, groceries, &c., taken, used, and destroyed by United States Army.
Susan B. Jackson, widow of John Jackson.
Illegal imprisonment and banishment of her husband and self, &c., by United States.
Cotton taken and sold by United States. Claim was on docket of Court of Claims.
Match factory and fixtures and stock burned by United States Army.
Match factory, fixtures, and stock burned, and’ horses, &c., taken by United States Army.
Saw and grist mill, and machinery, cotton, horses, mules, corn, bacon, sheep, cattle, &c., taken, used, and destroyed by United States Army.
Cotton burned by United States Army.
One mule, pork, beef, corn, and wheat, taken and used by United States Army.
Cotton burned on board steamship Blanche by United States steamship Montgomery.
Corn, wheat, and pork taken and used by United States Army.
Illegal imprisonment of self and wife by United States.
Horses, merchandise, clothing. &c., taken and used by United States Army.
Joseph B. Heycock, administrator of John Spinney.
Loss by capture and detention of schooner Argonaut by United States steamer Susquehanna. No appeal to Supreme Court.
Damage to cargo of schooner Argonaut captured by United States steamer Susquehanna. No appeal to Supreme Court.
Henry A. Moody, administrator of Henry Moody.
Cotton, flour, hogs, goats, poultry, fodder, &c., taken, used, and destroyed by United States Army.
Use of and damage to dwelling, two cows, vegetables, lumber, &c., taken and used by United States Army.
Brick-machine destroyed by United States Army; one mule, hay, corn, and hogs, taken and used by United States Army.
Charles Sabourin. Claimant died, and case, was revived in the name of Jane Sabourin, administratrix.
Cotton burned by United States Army
Horses, surgical instruments, medicines, and drugs, taken and used by United States Army.
Schooner Isabel and cargo of cotton seized by United States steamer J. L. Davis and sold by United States.
Steamship Pearl, seized by United States steamer Tioga and sold by United States.
Steamer Virgin, seized by the confederate authorities, and afterward captured by the United States.
Dismissed for want of jurisdiction Oct. 4, 1872.
Molasses taken, used, and destroyed by United States Army.

Engine, gearing, pumps, tools, shops, iron pipe, &c., destroyed and burned by the United States Army; molasses and sugar taken and used by United States Army; and illegal imprisonment by United States.
Detention of and damage to schooner D. F. Keeling by United States, and expenses incurred on account thereof. No appeal to the Supreme Court.
Emily J. Sherard, later Emily J. Gilland, administratrix of William Sherrard.
Stock of goods taken, used, and destroyed by United States Army.
Bricks, lumber, fence, posts, and 1 cow, taken and used by United States Army.
Corn, bacon, cattle, fodder, straw, rails, 1 horse, 1 mule, and dwelling-house, taken and used by United States.
27 vessels and their cargoes, captured by United States. No appeal to Supreme Court.
Timber and wood taken and used by United States Army.
Dwellings and out-buildings, fence, garden, &c., taken and used by United States Army.
For damage and detention of schooner Echo, and cargo by United States steamer Sunflower. No appeal to the Supreme Court.
Carriages, buggies, iron sate, promissory notes, account-books, &c., taken and destroyed by United States Army.
Furniture, clothing, &c., taken, used, and destroyed by United States Army.
74 coops of fowls seized by United States on bark Matilda.
Oliver K. King, administrator for William Stewart.
Damages and detention of the schooner Matamoras by United States steamer Virginia. No appeal to Supreme Court.
$24,447.39 int. and int. to date of payment.
Steamer Douro seized and detained by United States.

Sugar captured on brig John Welch by confederate privateer Jeff Davis, and sold by confederate government, proceeds held for claimant by confederate government and seized by United States, with other assets of said confederate government.
Ann Grayson, administratrix for John J. Cowley.
Cotton, broadcloth, jeans, shirts, clothing, &c., &c., taken, used, and destroyed by United States Army.
Brig Sarah Starr, captured by United States steamer Wabash and sold by United States, and for fees, demurrage, &c.
Disallowed, as to Sarah Starr and Aigburth, Nov. 8, 1872 the reasons for non-appeal being deemed insufficient.
Schooner Aigburth captured by United States steamer Jamestown and sold by United States, and for fees, demurrage, &c.
Wine destroyed by United States Army
Cotton burned by United States Army
Cotton burned by United States Army.
Cotton burned by United States Army. No appeal to Supreme Court.
Cotton seized and sold by United States; net proceeds received through the Court of Claims.
And int., and interest on $8,428.96 for 3 years.
Patterns of cutlery taken by United States Army.
Patterns of cutlery taken by United States Army.
Cotton burned, by United States Army
Horse, buggy, and harness taken and used by United States Army.
Herman Baer, administrator of Barry Drew Hewetson.
C. Eugenia O’Bryan, administratrix of Perry O’Bryan.
Cotton, sugar, oil, whisky, tobacco, beef, and coffee taken and used by United States. Cotton claim was on docket of Court of Claims.
Cotton burned by United States Army
For use and damage to Factor’s Cotton Press, Anchor Cotton Press, Louisiana Cotton Press, and Yards No. 1, 2, and 3, and stables, yards, quarters, &c. by United States.
Cotton seized and sold by United States. Now pending in the Court of Claims.
Withdrawn by Her Britannic Majesty’s agent, Feb. 4, 1873.
William Rose; became an United States citizen.
Cotton seized and sold by United States. Now pending in the Court of Claims.
Withdrawn by Her Britannic Majesty’s agent, Feb.4, 1873.
Emilia J. Pothier, executrix of L. B. Pothier.
Laura Armitage, wife of Robert Armitage; No. 348 vs. The United States.
Saddle, bridle, stirrups, needles, and thread, taken by United States Army.
Withdrawn by Her Britannic Majesty’s agent, Oct. 4, 1872.
For schooner Prince Leopold, seized and sold by United States, and $2,500 per annum, value of said vessel to claimant, from September 1, 1861. No appeal to Supreme Court.
Norman W. McColl, administrator of Hugh McColl.
Cypress lumber, machinery of sawmill, saws, axes, chains, blacksmith’s tools, carpenter’s tools, material, damage to buildings, &c., by United States Army.
Flour, pork, beef, sugar, coffee, furniture, account-books, library, tools, damage to house, suspension of business, &c., by United States Army.
Lucie F. Garrett, administratrix of Charles Fleming.
Two mules, two horses, ten cattle, building, fencing, material and implements in foundry, rope, &c., taken, used, and destroyed by United States gunboat Clifton.
Dismissed for want of juris, diction, June 10, 1873, claim-anthavingmarried an United States citizen, and there being no British beneficiaries.
Bacon, lard, sugar, wine, wool, &c., destroyed by United States Army.
Molasses taken by United States Army.
John C. Burden. Claimant died, and ease was revived in the name of Emma G. Burden, administratrix.
Property taken and damage to house

Four hogsheads sugar and three bales of cotton.
(Taken, used, and destroyed by United States Army.)

Whisky, tobacco, rubber-bands, drags, oil, hardware, clothing, dry-goods, furniture, books, stationery, saddles, harness, hogs, poultry, and money, &, taken, used, and destroyed by United States Army.
Cotton seized by United States Army.
Dismissed for want of jurisdiction, without prejudice to the prosecution of the claim elsewhere, Jan. 20, 1873.
Cotton seized by United States Army. Claim pending in the Court of Claims.
Quicksilver mine from which claimants were ejected by United States.
Damage for steamer Monmouth being warned off the coast of all the Southern States by United States steamer Niagara.
For cargo of Springbok, captured by United States steamer Sonoma.
Costs and expenses incurred by claimants.

Cotton seized and sold, by the United States.
Claim on docket of Court of Claims.
Part of claim withdrawn Nov. 27, 1872, and rest dismissed without prejudice to the prosecution of the claim elsewhere, April 30, 1873.
Cotton seized and sold by United States. Now pending in Court of Claims.
Claim withdrawn by Her Britannic Majesty’s agent, Nov. 27, 1872.
Elizabeth L. H. Bowie; married a United States citizen.
Tobacco factory and fixtures destroyed by United States Army; tobacco, horses, mules, Hour, meal, bacon, &c., &c., taken and used by United States Army.
Tobacco burned at Richmond, Va., by reason of the United States restraining claimant from removing the same.
Tobacco burned at Richmond, Va., by reason of the United States restraining claimant from removing the same.
Eliza H. Molyneux, executrix of Edmund Molyuenx.
Injury to dwelling, furniture, &c., of claimant, and cotton taken by United State Army.
George, Henry, and Jas. W. B. Money.
For depreciation in Bank of Louisiana stock by the “civil war.”
For depreciation of bank-notes taken by officers of United States steamer Dan Smith.
Illegal imprisonment by United States Army.
Furniture, &c., burned by United States Army.
Expenses in removing to place of safety
Cotton and turpentine seized by United States Army.
Rosin burned by United States Army. Claim for cotton and turpentine; was on docket of Court of Claims.
Cotton burned by United States Army.
Cotton burned by United States Army.
Cotton burned by United States Army.
William G. Ford, administrator of J. G. Robinson.
Cotton seized and sold by United States.
Award $29,683 for the benefit of Mary G. Barker, or her representatives, Sept 24, 1873.
John Q. Watkins and Bernard Donnelly, administrators of John Shannon.
Dry goods taken by United States Army. Claim was first filed in the name of Samuel Jarboe, guardian, &c.
Sugar, molasses, bed and dress clothes, one mule, cotton, tobacco, &c., &c., taken and used by United States Army.
Timber taken and used by United States.
Clothing, silk, and satin dresses, &c., lost while in charge of the United States Army.

Iron and brass foundery, machinery, shops, lumber, wood, coal, oil-cloth, fee, destroyed by United States Army; two horses, harness, and buggy, &c., taken and used by United States Army; and damage to dwelling, orchard, &c., by United States Army.
Cotton burned by United States Army.
For rent of mansion-house and premises called “Rosslyn;” damage to same, loss of crops, and destruction of dwelling by fire while in possession of United States, and interest to December 31, 1871.
Two horses; wagon, harness, apple brandy, meal, corn, flour, bacon, quilts, fee, taken and used by United States Army.
Current money, four horses, wagon, harness, clothing, and oats taken and used by United States Army.
Gold, clothes, wheat, oats, saddle, fee, and damages by United States Army.
Cotton burned by rebel scouts, by neglect of the United States Army.
Cotton burned by United States Army.
Saw and grist mill and machinery, fee, burned by United States Army; and grain, flour, books clothes, fee, taken by United States Army.
Two cases books seized by United States Army.
Mary A. Barrow, administratrix of George Barrow.
Illegal imprisonment of her husband by United States, and goods, books, and papers taken, used, and destroyed by United States Army.
For furnishing food and lodgings to United States officers and soldiers, and provender for United States horses.
Jos. Fry Mogridge. (Was born in the United States.)
Dry-goods, glass-ware, stationery, groceries, drugs, medicines, liquors, &c., taken, used, and destroyed by United States Army.
Dismissed for want of jurisdiction Nov. 18, 1872.
William H. Stewart, executor of Chas. E. Stewart.
Cotton burned by United States Army.
Loss on forced sale of horses, carriages, and removal of wine, glass, plate, and destruction by bombardment of furniture, china, &c.
Two last items withdrawn March 26, 1872; rest disallowed Jan. 23, 1873.
Personal property and valuables taken by United States Army.
Loss on sale of dwelling-house and furniture, rope, twine, needles, leather, &c., taken by United States Army.
Cotton seized and sold by United States. Claim in Court of Claims.
Dismissed for want of jurisdiction, without prejudice to the prosecution of the claim elsewhere, Jan. 20 1873.
Books, manuscripts, wearing apparel, mineralogical specimens, gold, and copper, &c., destroyed by United States Army.
Trunk of books, music, and clothing, left with Her Britannic Majesty’s vice-consul and destroyed.
John Butterworth and John Howorth, executors of Henry Kay.
Cotton burned by United States Army.
Loss on cotton seized by United States
For restitution of tax on sugar, molasses, and cotton, paid to the United States.
Cotton part seized and part destroyed by United States Army.
Cotton part seized and part destroyed by United States Army.
Sugar and molasses destroyed by act of United States Army.
Sugar destroyed by act of United States Army.
Cotton destroyed by act of United States Army.
Loss on cotton seized by United States, and expenses incurred thereby.
Lumber, wheelbarrows, shovels, mattresses, blankets, cooking utensils, &c., taken and used by United States Army.
Loss on contract for building levees, &c.
Edward McHugh, administrator of James McHugh.
Illegal imprisonment of his son by the United States.
Loss on goods at Bermuda in consequence of son’s imprisonment.
Electro-plated ware, shipped and received at the custom-house, and lost.
Elizabeth Sherman, administratrix of Thomas F. Sherman, alias Thomas J. Miller.
Abduction and illegal imprisonment of her husband by United States.
Martha M. Calderwood, executrix of John Calderwood.
Cotton taken and destroyed by United States Army.
Cotton, mules, horses, corn, furniture, silver-plate, cattle, and meat, taken and used by United States Army.
Sugar, molasses, horses, buggy, corn, and cow taken and used by United States Army.
Goods and stores seized by United States Army.

Cotton, tobacco, sugar, molasses, flour, rosin, turpentine, coffee, chiccory, rice, salt, tea, raisins, spices, medicines, oils, whisky, gin, rum, brandy, ale, porter, cigars, &c., and two ship’s boats, supposed to have been taken, used, and destroyed by United States Army.
Georgia, Alabama, Mississippi, Louisiana, Tennessee, South Carolina, Kentucky, Virginia, and North Carolina.

The claim for cotton, &c., and so much of claim for imprisonment which was after the 9th of April, 1865, was withdrawn by the claimant July 7, 1873; the rest of the claim was disallowed Sept 17, 1873.
Cotton seized and sold by United States, now pending in Court of Claims.
Dismissed for want of jurisdiction, without prejudice, Nov 29, 1872.
Cotton seized by United States, now pending in Court of Claims.
Dismissed for want of jurisdiction, without prejudice, Nov 29, 1872.
Cotton seized and sold by United States. Net proceeds received through the Court of Claims.
Dismissed for want of jurisdiction, without prejudice, Nov 29, 1872.
Cotton seized by United States, now pending in Court of Claims.
Dismissed for want of jurisdiction, without prejudice, Nov 29, 1872.
Cotton seized and sold by United States. Net proceeds received through the Court of Claims.
Dismissed for want of jurisdiction, without prejudice, Nov 29, 1872.
Steamer M. S. Perry, captured by United States steamer Keystone State. No appeal to Supreme Court.
Cotton burned by United States Army.
Cotton seized and sold by United States. Net proceeds received through Court of Claims.
Dismissed for want of jurisdiction, without prejudice, Jan 7, 1873.
Cotton seized and sold by United States. Now pending in Court of Claims.
Dismissed for want of jurisdiction, without prejudice, Jan 7, 1873.
Cotton seized an by sold by United States. Net proceeds received through Court of Claims.
Dismissed for want of jurisdiction, without prejudice, Jan 7, 1873.
Cotton seized and sold by United States. Now pending in Court of Claims.
Dismissed for want of jurisdiction, without prejudice, Jan 7, 1873.
Cotton burned by United States Army.
Steamer Granite City and cargo, captured by United States gun-boat Tioga. No appeal to Supreme Court.
Owners of the cargo of Will-o-the-Wisp.
For cargo of the Will-o-the-Wisp, captured by the United States steamer Montgomery. No appeal to Supreme Court.
William H. Fisher, in re Minnie.
For vessel Minnie, &c., captured by the United States steamer Lodona. No appeal to Supreme Court.
Wines, tobacco, soap, allspice, water-coolers, goods in his store, &c., taken and used by United States Army.
Cotton burned by United States Army.
Cotton burned by United States Army.
Cotton burned by United States Army.
Cotton burned by United States Army; loss on cotton seized by United States and afterwards restored to claimants.
Land confiscated and sold by United States.
For detention of steamship Labuan by United States.
For bark Empress, captured by United States steamer Vincennes.
John Amy, trustee, &c., et al., for heirs of M. Gallichan, for T. C. Le Gross, for E. LeConteur, and for J. LeCras.
Illegal capture and detention of the brig Volant by United States steamer Virginia.
Off mouth of the Rio Grande.
Henry Lafone and John T. Lawrence.
Steamer Sunbeam and cargo, captured by United States steamer State of Georgia, and sold by United States.
Steamer Eagle and cargo, captured by United States steamer Octorora, and sold by United States.
Steamer Greyhound and cargo, captured by United States steamer Connecticut, and sold by United States.
Steamer Lilian, captured by United States steamer Keystone and sold by United States.
Steamer Lucy and cargo, captured by United States steamer Santiago, and sold by United States.

Steamer Emma Henry and cargo, captured by United States steamer Cherokee, and sold by United States.
Also for the net proceeds obtained for the cargo of the steamer Lilian. No appeal to Supreme Court.
Walter Easton, trustee for J. G. Galbraith, S. J. Redgate, and J. H. Elsworth.
For loss sustained by the illegal seizure of the brig Geziena Helligonda by United States steamer Pembina.
Thomas Elliot Angel et al., owners of the bark Science.
For bark Science and cargo, captured and sold by United States.
A. B. Forwood and James Dorrington, owners of Adela.
For steamship Adela, captured by United States steamers Quaker City and Huntsville, and sold by United States.
For cargo of steamship Adela, captured by United States steamers Quaker City and Huntsville, and sold by United States.
Charles LeQuesne et al., owners of brig Dashing Wave.
For brig Dashing Wave, captured by United States’ steamers Owasco and Virginia, and sold by United States.
Off mouth of the Rio Grande.
For part of cargo Dashing Wave, captured by United States steamers Owasco and Virginia, and sold by United States.
Off mouth of the Rio Grande.
For part of cargo Dashing Wave, captured by United States steamers Owasco and Virginia, and sold by United States.
Off mouth of the Rio Grande.
For bark Hiawatha, captured by United States steamer Minnesota, and sold by United States.
Ezekiel McLeod, assignee, &c., for Thomas Potts.
For bark Hiawatha, captured by United States steamer Minnesota, and sold by United States.
For part of cargo Hiawatha, captured by United States steamer Minnesota, and sold by United States.
For part of cargo Hiawatha, captured by United States steamer Minnesota, and sold by United States.
For part of cargo Hiawatha, captured by United States steamer Minnesota, and sold by United States.
Tobacco burned and otherwise lost and destroyed, by reason of the United States restraining claimant from removing the same.
Jos. Spence and Geo. Fleming, owners steamship Peterhoff.

For steamship Peterhoff, captured by United States steamer Vanderbilt, and sold by the United States; the cost price of said steamship, and profits on freight; and interest on £16,000, at £5 per centum per annum.
For part of cargo Peterhoff, captured by United States steamer Vanderbilt, and sold by United States.
For part of cargo Peterhoff, captured by United States steamer Vanderbilt, and sold by United States.
For part of cargo Peterhoff, captured by United States steamer Vanderbilt, and sold by United States.
Wilson, Bowles & Co., for Ford, Curtis & Curtis.
For part of cargo Peterhoff, captured by United States steamer Vanderbilt and sold by United States.
For part of cargo Peterhoff, captured by United States steamer Vanderbilt and sold by United States.
For part of cargo Peterhoff, captured by United States steamer Vanderbilt and sold by United States.
For part of cargo Peterhoff, captured by United States steamer Vanderbilt, and sold by United States.

For part, of cargo Peterhoff, captured by United States steamer Vanderbilt, and sold by United States.
For part of cargo Peterhoff, captured by United States steamer Vanderbilt, and sold by United States.
For part of cargo Peterhoff, captured by United States steamer Vanderbilt, and sold by United States.
For part of cargo Peterhoff, captured by United States steamer Vanderbilt, and sold by United States.

Robert Bowden passenger on the Peterhoff.
Illegal imprisonment by the United States.
Samuel Redgate, passenger on the Peterhoff.
Illegal imprisonment by the United States.
John H. Ellsworth, passenger on the Peterhoff.
Illegal imprisonment by the United States, and damages consequent upon the breaking up of his business.
For part of cargo Peterhoff, captured by United States steamer Vanderbilt, and sold by United States.
And £5 pr. ct. per ann. int. and costs.
For part of cargo Peterhoff, captured by United States steamer Vanderbilt, and sold by United States.
For part of cargo Peterhoff, captured by United States steamer Vanderbilt, and sold by United States.
For part of cargo Peterhoff, captured by United States steamer Vanderbilt, and sold by United States.
For part of cargo Peterhoff, captured by United States steamer Vanderbilt, and sold by United States.
Thames and Mersey Marine Insurance Comp’y, (limited.)
For insurance on cargo of the Hashing Wave, captured by the United States steamers Owasco and Virginia, and sold by United States. Net proceeds received through act of Congress.
British and Foreign Marine Insurance Company, limited, and John Young and James Bird, liquidators of the English and Scottish Marine Insurance Company.
For insurance on cargo of the Dashing Wave, captured by United States steamers Owasco and Virginia, and sold by United States. Net proceeds received through act of Congress.
For steamship Georgia, captured by United States steamer Niagara, and sold by United States.
Henry Lafone and John T. Lawrence
Cotton taken and sold by United States. Claim in the Court of Claims
Dismissed for want of jurisdiction, without prejudice to prosecution of the claim elsewhere, April 19, 1873.
For steamer Banshee, captured by United States steamers Grand Gulf and Fulton, and sold by United States.
For steamer Tristram Shandy and cargo, captured by United States steamer Kansas, and sold by United States. No appeal to Supreme Court.
For steamship Circassian, captured by United States steamer Somerset, and sold by United States.
& £5 per ct. per ann. and costs.
For the value of the outward freight of the steamship Circassian, captured by United States steamer Somerset, and sold by United States.
For ship York seized and burned by United States steamers Brussalero and Albatross.
For real estate confiscated by United States.
For illegal imprisonment, and horse and saddle taken from him by United States.
Spinning-frames destroyed by United States Army.
Mules, wagons, calicoes, muslins, cloth, laces, needles, combs, &, taken by United States Army.
One mule and 1 bale of cotton taken and used by United States Army.
Five mules, wagons, clothing, jewelry, household articles, &, taken by United States Army.
Watch, pistol, saddle, bag of cotton, &, taken by United States Army.
For schooner Adelso seized and sold by United States. No appeal to Supreme Court.
For part of cargo Peterhoff captured by United States steamer Vanderbilt and sold by United States. Net proceeds received through United States Supreme Court.
Walter Easton, trustee, &, for John Graham Galbraith.
For part of cargo Peterhoff captured by United States steamer Vanderbilt and sold by United States. Net proceeds received through United States Supreme Court.
For part of cargo Peterhoff captured by United States steamer Vanderbilt and sold by United States. Net proceeds received through United States Supreme Court.
And 5 per ct. per annum interest and costs.
For part of cargo Peterhoff captured by United States steamer Vanderbilt and sold by United States. Net proceeds received through United States Supreme Court.
And 5 per ct. per annum interest and costs.
John Riley, manager of insurance company, and assignee of Thomas May.
For bark Springbok captured by United States steamer Sonoma.
Insurance companies and underwriters at Lloyds.
For cargo of Circassian captured by United States steamer Somerset.
Janet Mason, administratrix of Wm. C. Beattie.
Derricks, harness, drill, shovels, picks, lumber, steel, wagons, and blacksmith’s tools taken, used, and destroyed by United States Army.
Elizabeth Brain, executrix of John Brain.
Illegal imprisonment of her husband by United States.
Hats, caps, boots, and shoes, taken and used by United States Army.
For schooner James Douglas, found abandoned at sea by United States steamer Monticello, and afterward used by the United States.
Damage to store-house by United States Army.
Dismissed without prejudice and for want of jurisdiction March 22, 1873.
One horse, wagon, harness, saddles, hogs, sugar, corn-beef, bacon, cows, calves, kitchen furniture, tableware, &c., taken, used, and destroyed by the United States Army.
24 head of cattle and 400 bushels of wheat, taken and used by United States Army.
Jas. Faill and John M. McCulloch, executors of Charles McEwen.
Tobacco, part of cargo of the Hiawatha, captured by United States steamer Minnesota, and sold by United States.
For detention of schooner Jane Campbell, and cargo and stores used by United States.
Timber cut, rails taken or destroyed, rent of farm; sheep, horses, cattle, wheat, corn, bacon, hay, oats, houses, &c., taken, used, and destroyed by confederate army.
Sugar, cotton, and $865 in gold taken by United States Army.
For being driven from her home, and the death of three of her children from want and exposure, by United States Army.
Two cows and calves, and household furniture, taken, used, and destroyed by United States Army.
One horse, one head of cattle, shot-gun, clothing and jewelry, taken and used by United States Army.
And loss on forced sale of farm
Cotton burned by United States Army.
Cotton burned by United States Army.
Use and destruction by fire of a tobacco-stemmery, &c., by United States Army.
Household furniture, clothing, library, horse and buggy, wine and liquors taken, used, and destroyed by United States Army.
Withdrawn by her Britannic Majesty’s agent Mar 17, 1873.
Office furniture, account-books, &c., taken and destroyed by United States Army.
Illegal imprisonment by United States on board Dashing Wave.
Cotton part damaged and part burned by States Army.
For loss on bark Hilja, being warned off entering blockaded ports by United States steamer Niagara.
Claim withdrawn by Her Britannic Majesty’s agent December 17, 1872.
Wood and lumber taken and used by United States Army.
Damage to dwelling, out-houses, trees, &c. by United States Army.
Two horses, two mules, and eleven tons of hay taken and used by United States Army.
Elizabeth Bennett, executrix of Benjamin Bennett.
Three horses, hogs, sheep, cattle, corn, &e, taken and used by United States Army.
Occupation of farm by United States Army.
Cotton, horses, mule, wagon, and dress and undress goods taken, used, and destroyed by United States Army.
Osnaburgs and cotton yarns taken by United States Army.
Two mules and 130 head of cattle taken and used by United States Army.
Cotton destroyed by the United States Army and the confederate army.
First National Bank of St. Albans.
United States Treasury and State bank notes, &c., taken and carried off by the raiders.
For illegal imprisonment and wounding of claimant by the raiders.
William Fuller and Erasmus D. Fuller.
One horse lost, one horse used, and damages to other horses, saddles, bridles, halters by the raiders, and expenses incurred by claimants in pursuing their property.
Bradley Barlow, receiver, &c., of the Saint Albans Bank.
United States legal tender, State bank bills, and specie taken and carried off by the raiders.
Mariaette Field, administratrix of Sylvester Field.
Injury to horses, loss of saddles, bridles, and halters taken by the raiders, and expenses incurred by claimant in recovering the same.
7–30 Treasury notes taken by the raiders from the Saint Albans bank.
Money belonging to claimant taken from the Saint Albans bank by the raiders.
Samuel Breck and Jonathan Wetherbee, jr.
State and national bank bills taken by the raiders, and expense incurred in pursuing their property.
Bonds, with coupons attached, taken from First National Bank by the raiders, and expense incurred in pursuing his property.
United States coupon bonds taken from First National Bank by the raiders.
For detention of ship Daring by Great Britain at Calcutta.
For detention of ship Templar by Great Britain at Calcutta.
Bills of the Franklin County Bank, United States legal-tender notes, and other State bank bills taken and carried off by the raiders.
Lucien B. Clough, administrator of Elinas J. Morrison.
For the shooting and killing of E. J. Morrison by the raiders.
For expulsion from Island of San Juan and consequent loss of lime-quarry, &c.
For detention of bark Patmos by Great Britain at Calcutta.
As inventor of breech-loading firearms, adopted by Her Britannic Majesty’s Government.
Francis Curtis and Samuel E. Peabody.
For interest on goods shipped on ship Daring, detained at Calcutta.
Illegal imprisonment by the Johnson Island raiders.
Personal clothing stolen by the raiders.
Damage to the Philo Parsons, furniture destroyed, blankets, money, &c., stolen, &c.
Damage to passengers on the Philo Parsons in the loss of clothing stolen by the raiders.
Damage to owners of cargo of the Philo Parsons by the raiders.
Damages sustained by the steamer Island Queen Stock Company by the raiders.
